text:
Time is the invisible current we swim through, noticed most acutely when we're fighting against it or when it suddenly slips away. We measure it obsessively—in seconds, seasons, years—as if precision could somehow give us more of it, yet the more we count, the faster it seems to go.

There's something paradoxical about how we experience duration. A moment of genuine joy can vanish in what feels like an instant, while waiting for something dreaded stretches endlessly. A year fills with ordinary days that blur together, yet when you look back, the whole period has condensed into a handful of memorable moments. We live as if we have infinite time, then suddenly find ourselves at a point where that assumption feels dangerously foolish.

Time also transforms us without our noticing. The person you were five years ago seems like a stranger until you catch your reflection and realize you're aging at the same constant rate you always were. We change gradually enough that we don't feel the shift happening, yet we arrive somewhere completely different from where we started.

Perhaps the deepest thing about time is that it's the one resource we truly cannot renew. Money comes and goes, skills can be relearned, relationships can be repaired, but the hours we spend are gone forever. This should terrify us more than it does, yet most of us spend our time as carelessly as if we had an inexhaustible supply. The wisdom, it seems, isn't in seizing every moment or optimizing every hour, but in occasionally pausing to notice that time is passing at all—and choosing, with that awareness, what deserves to fill it.
